chunks.title: Vioxx Study Controversy
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses their experience participating in a Vioxx trial, revealing the manipulation of the patient population to skew results. They highlight the risk of serious gastrointestinal complications that were overlooked for those not on corticosteroids, ultimately leading to the drug's flawed approval process.
chunks.transcript: but I participated in one of the last vioxx trials I think it was 2011. and um in that study that I was talking about before the bigger study that was designed to show that Vox caused fewer GI complications they rigged the patient population so that 55 percent of the people who were in that study were taking corticosteroids like Prednisone for arthritis now that's in a normal population less than three percent of the people are taking corticosteroids uh in conjunction with anti-inflammatory medicines but in this study 55 more than half were taking corticosteroids plus an anti-inflammatory drug so I realized that Merck hadn't submitted data on the risk of serious GI complications in that study among the people who weren't taking prednisone at the same time or prednisone like drug and because we're in litigation I could ask the lawyers to get the data from Merck on this the raw data in a form form that a statistician could analyze in those great statistician that I was working with and we did that we got the data and we found that for people who were not taking steroids concurrently biox was no gentler on the stomach it was all nonsense except for the very small percentage of people who are taking viox plus an anti-inflammatory an over-the-counter anti-inflammatory drug and nobody knew nobody had figured that out I mean there's a whole lot of experts including myself had been involved in this for years and nobody had figured it out until the very end so we we did that and the statistician wrote it up in a hyper geeky statistical journal and nobody knows that fact I mean my telling you right now is going to significantly increase the number of people who know that that the study was designed for on a population that wasn't going to be like the population that the drug was going to be prescribed for

chunks.title: Current Drug Approval Issues
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to recent controversies around Alzheimer's drugs approved by the FDA despite evidence of ineffectiveness and potential harm. The discussion includes insights on the conflicts of interest within the FDA and how this reflects ongoing issues in the approval of pharmaceutical drugs.
chunks.transcript: do you think this is still happening today I mean a lot of this was in the early 2000s and have you heard about this this drug that's been marketed for the last I think four or five years for um Alzheimer's that uh that it changes beta amyloid in the brain and it doesn't it doesn't help at all and I think that the probably it's probably like vioxides I'd have to go look at the raw data but it actually causes severe side effects and doesn't really help because we know that the whole amyloid hypothesis with Alzheimer's is wrong and that having an antibody that gets rid of you know amyloid beta in the brain is probably doing much more harm than it is good but it's still approved by the FDA and it's still prescribed like what is going on it's crazy it's crazy yeah that's the PostScript to my book and it's in sickening at the very end I added that in the last minute because for exactly the reason that you're bringing this up it's still going on and it's just craziness the the FDA advisory committee voted 10 to nothing with one abstention not to approve this drug that it did more harm than good these are the independent doctors that come into uh evaluate so they vote 10 to nothing with one abstention not to approve it and the FDA approves it three of the doctors quit who are on the advisory committee because they're so rich I know one of the guys is a really good guy is really smart so the woman who's in charge of the I think the center for drug evaluation and research at Pfizer who excuse me at the FDA though probably at five she had spent 16 years as an executive in the pharmaceutical industry I don't know if she was advisor that was a slip but the the woman who oversaw this process um had spent 16 years as an executive for Pharma before she came to the FDA so her comment on this this just blunder that is so huge that you can't even make up an analogy for how stupid it was for the FDA to approve

chunks.title: Nutritional Guidelines and Conflicts of Interest
chunks.summary: The speaker addresses the 2020-2025 nutritional guidelines and highlights that a significant majority of the committee members had ties to industry, raising concerns about the influence of food and pharmaceutical industries on health recommendations. This is linked to a broader pattern of conflicts of interest in nutritional recommendations.
chunks.transcript: to allow conflicts of interest with drug companies amongst the FDA advisory committee members because then I'm not making this up because then there won't be so many emotional reactions and that's how she's going to fix the problem so conflicts of interest are okay we're just we're just good because they wouldn't be Discord in the advisory committee because everyone would vote to approve the drug you know it's crazy that the amount of conflicts of interest in medicine at large are striking to me um my friend Andrew huberman sent me something recently that I posted about for the 2020 to 2025 nutritional guidelines for the United States and there was a paper published saying that 95 19 of the 20 people on the committee that made the 2020 to 2025 nutritional guidelines had ties to Industry Pharma food agriculture things like ilsee the international Life Sciences Institute which is widely regarded as kind of a

chunks.title: Statins and the Pharmaceutical Industry
chunks.summary: The discussion transitions to statins, with an emphasis on their widespread use and the educational bias in medicine that promotes drug solutions over lifestyle changes. The speaker emphasizes the absence of comparative studies between healthy lifestyle modifications and statin use, highlighting the influence of pharmaceutical funding in medical research.
chunks.transcript: foods um and so like 19 out of the 20 people had had industry ties and and I did a com I did a you know a social media post about that and then if you look at and we can talk about statins briefly if you look at the the 2003-2004 AHA guidelines right to change the levels of LDL I think it was 8 out of 10 people had ties to the Statin industry let's talk about that one because that's the Statin one I did a whole podcast on statins previously but statins are really um they they really grind my gears right well they should because it's a perfect example of why American medicine is spending so much money and yet not getting as good a results as the other wealthy countries and the issue is that all the data and all the guidelines are designed to sell drugs and so virtually all of the clinical trials that are done um are funded by the the drug companies uh they do not make their data available they make it available to an outfit in Oxford that puts all the data together but they're they have their ties to the drug companies too and here's the thing that the research about how to prevent cardiovascular disease is dominated by statins and now more expensive drugs are coming along that's what our research is about and that's what medical students learn about and that's what doctors learn about but they're learning about what drugs might benefit in the reduction of the risk of cardiovascular disease they are not learning about what they think they're learning about they think they're learning about how they can help their patients prevent heart disease they're not learning about that nobody's ever done a study that compares healthy lifestyle modification to statins that that study's not been done why hasn't it been done because the people who would fund it the drug companies can only lose they've got the whole thing in their pocket there's no reason to gamble with uh losing their
